title: Stable Selection Sort with Incorrect Comparison

statement.markdown: Implement a stable selection sort algorithm, which sorts an array of integers in-place. The algorithm should work correctly even when the input array is nearly sorted.
statement.inputFormat: The method will be passed an array of integers as input. The array will contain n elements where 1 <= n <= 10000.
statement.outputFormat: The method should sort the input array in-place.
statement.notes: Time complexity: O(n^2), where n is the size of the array.

examples.explanation: After sorting, the array should be in non-decreasing order.

hints.text: Think about the time complexity of the algorithm.
Consider the use of a temporary array for swapping.

editorial.title: Editorial
editorial.intuition: The algorithm should work by repeatedly finding the minimum element from the unsorted part and putting it at the beginning.
editorial.bruteForce: You can use the brute force approach by finding the minimum element from the unsorted part and swapping it with the first unsorted element.
editorial.betterSolution: Use a temporary array to swap elements.
editorial.optimalSolution: Use a single pass through the array to find the minimum element and swap it with the first unsorted element.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proven by showing that it satisfies the properties of a sorting algorithm.
commonMistakes: Comparing two consecutive elements instead of the first unsorted element and the first element of the unsorted part.
